vulnerabilities.title: Isolate Liquidation Bid Escrow Underflow Blocks Liquidations
vulnerabilities.summary: In executeIsolateLiquidate(), when an NFT's accrued debt (borrowAmount) exceeds the last bid amount (bidAmount) — which naturally occurs when interest accrues between auction and liquidation — the function attempts to subtract totalBorrowAmount from the bid escrow (assetData.totalBidAmout) via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity(). However, the escrow only holds sum(bidAmount_i) minus any remainBidAmounts already refunded. Since totalBorrowAmount > totalBidAmount when debt exceeds bids, this cau
vulnerabilities.impact: Isolate loan liquidations are permanently blocked whenever debt accrues beyond the last bid amount during the auction duration period. This is a common scenario since interest continuously accrues. Blocked liquidations cause bad debt accumulation, threatening protocol solvency and all depositors' funds.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: In executeIsolateLiquidate(), when an NFT's accrued debt (borrowAmount) exceeds the last bid amount (bidAmount) — which naturally occurs when interest accrues between auction and liquidation — the function attempts to subtract totalBorrowAmount from the bid escrow (assetData.totalBidAmout) via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity(). However, the escrow only holds sum(bidAmount_i) minus any remainBidAmounts already refunded. Since totalBorrowAmount > totalBidAmount when debt exceeds bids, this causes an arithmetic underflow revert at VaultLogic.sol line 497.

vulnerabilities.title: BVault.withdrawERC20 Uses Stale Amount for Native Token Unwrapping
vulnerabilities.summary: In BVault.withdrawERC20(), when withdrawing native tokens, the original `amount` parameter is passed to VaultLogic.unwrapNativeTokenInWallet() on line 74. However, SupplyLogic.executeWithdrawERC20() may cap the actual withdrawal to the user's balance (line 52-53: if userBalance < params.amount, params.amount = userBalance). The local `amount` variable in BVault remains unchanged, so unwrapNativeTokenInWallet tries to transferFrom the user an amount larger than what was actually withdrawn.
vulnerabilities.impact: Native token (ETH) withdrawals revert whenever the requested amount exceeds the user's actual deposit balance, including the common type(uint256).max pattern used to withdraw full balance. Users cannot withdraw native ETH through the normal withdrawal flow in these cases. They would need to withdraw as WETH (non-native) and unwrap manually.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: In BVault.withdrawERC20(), when withdrawing native tokens, the original `amount` parameter is passed to VaultLogic.unwrapNativeTokenInWallet() on line 74. However, SupplyLogic.executeWithdrawERC20() may cap the actual withdrawal to the user's balance (line 52-53: if userBalance < params.amount, params.amount = userBalance). The local `amount` variable in BVault remains unchanged, so unwrapNativeTokenInWallet tries to transferFrom the user an amount larger than what was actually withdrawn.

vulnerabilities.title: Isolate Redeem Decreases Wrong User's Borrow Balance
vulnerabilities.summary: In executeIsolateRedeem(), line 339 calls erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled), decreasing the isolate borrow balance of params.msgSender. However, the actual borrower is tokenData.owner (the NFT owner who took the isolate loan). There is no validation that params.msgSender == borrower. If another user who has their own isolate borrow in the same debt group calls redeem on someone else's auctioned NFT, their own borrow balance is reduced instead of
vulnerabilities.impact: Debt accounting corruption: the original borrower's debt remains unchanged while the redeemer's separate debt is reduced. The loan's scaledAmount is reduced (line 357) but the borrower's userScaledIsolateBorrow is not. This creates phantom debt that can never be repaid through normal channels. In practice, the redeemer also pays totalRedeemAmount into the pool (line 368), so no funds are directly stolen, but the accounting divergence can cascade into further issues including incorrect health factor calculations and blocked operations for the original borrower.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: In executeIsolateRedeem(), line 339 calls erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled), decreasing the isolate borrow balance of params.msgSender. However, the actual borrower is tokenData.owner (the NFT owner who took the isolate loan). There is no validation that params.msgSender == borrower. If another user who has their own isolate borrow in the same debt group calls redeem on someone else's auctioned NFT, their own borrow balance is reduced instead of the actual borrower's.

vulnerabilities.title: Flash Loan Bypasses Reentrancy Guard Enabling Stale-State Operations
vulnerabilities.summary: flashLoanERC20 intentionally omits the nonReentrant modifier (FlashLoan.sol line 19 comment). Since all modules execute via delegatecall from PoolManager, the reentrancyLock (stored in PoolManager's storage) remains UNLOCKED during the flash loan callback. This allows the callback to reenter any nonReentrant-protected function. Additionally, erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan do NOT update availableLiquidity, so during the callback, availableLiquidity is stale (reflects p
vulnerabilities.impact: During the flash loan callback, an attacker can call any protocol function (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay, liquidate) while availableLiquidity is stale. However, actual token transfers provide a natural backstop — safeTransfer reverts if the contract lacks sufficient balance. This limits the practical severity: the attacker cannot extract more tokens than the contract holds. The primary risk is in operations that rely on availableLiquidity for business logic decisions (e.g., interest rate calculations, borrow limits) rather than token transfers. Interest rate manipulation during flash loans could provide MEV-style advantages.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: flashLoanERC20 intentionally omits the nonReentrant modifier (FlashLoan.sol line 19 comment). Since all modules execute via delegatecall from PoolManager, the reentrancyLock (stored in PoolManager's storage) remains UNLOCKED during the flash loan callback. This allows the callback to reenter any nonReentrant-protected function. Additionally, erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan do NOT update availableLiquidity, so during the callback, availableLiquidity is stale (reflects pre-flash-loan state).
